questions.q: Identify the four behaviours that any wave can show.
questions.explanation: Any wave can reflect, refract, diffract and superpose. Superposition is the parent of interference, standing waves and resonance. (Conduction, convection and radiation are the three heat-transfer modes, not wave behaviours.)

questions.q: A sound particle's displacement-time graph shows a period of 2 ms, in air where v = 340 m s⁻¹. Calculate the wavelength.
questions.explanation: From the time graph, f = 1/T = 1/0.002 = 500 Hz. Then λ = v/f = 340/500 = 0.68 m. The time graph gives the period (and frequency); the wave equation then gives the wavelength — the standard two-step recipe.

questions.q: Light travels from glass (n = 1.5) toward a glass-air boundary. Calculate the critical angle (sin iᶜ = 1/1.5 = 0.67).
questions.explanation: sin iᶜ = n₂/n₁ = 1/1.5 ≈ 0.67, so iᶜ = sin⁻¹(0.67) ≈ 42°. A ray striking above 42° (and going from denser glass to less dense air) undergoes total internal reflection. (Water's critical angle to air is the larger ~49°, since water has a lower index.)

options: Heat is the average particle energy; temperature is transferred energy
Temperature is the average particle kinetic energy; heat is energy transferred due to a temperature difference
They are the same quantity in different units
Heat is measured in kelvin; temperature in joules
questions.explanation: Temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of the particles (a property an object has); heat is energy transferred from a hotter to a colder object because of a temperature difference (energy in transit). An object contains internal energy, not heat. Treating them as synonyms is the classic error.

questions.q: Heat is conducted through a glass window: k = 0.8, area 2 m², ΔT = 15 K, thickness 0.004 m. Calculate the rate of heat flow (Q/t = kAΔT/d).
questions.explanation: Q/t = kAΔT/d = (0.8 × 2 × 15)/0.004 = 24/0.004 = 6000 W. The large rate is why single-glazed windows lose so much heat — double glazing adds a low-conductivity air gap to cut it. Conduction is faster for high k, large area, large ΔT and small thickness.

questions.q: An organ pipe open at both ends is 0.68 m long (v = 340 m s⁻¹). Sounded with a 254 Hz fork, calculate the beat frequency.
questions.explanation: Open-pipe fundamental: λ₀ = 2L = 1.36 m, so f₀ = v/λ₀ = 340/1.36 = 250 Hz. Beat frequency = |254 − 250| = 4 beats per second. This layers three skills — pipe rule, wave equation, then beats.

questions.q: Identify the single heat-transfer mode that can cross the vacuum of space, and how it relates to waves.
options: Radiation, because it is electromagnetic waves needing no medium
Convection, because hot gas rises in space
All three can cross a vacuum
questions.explanation: Only radiation can cross a vacuum — it is electromagnetic radiation (the same family as light) and needs no medium, which is how the Sun's heat reaches the Earth. Conduction needs a material and convection needs a fluid, so neither works in a vacuum.

questions.q: A lamp gives an intensity of 36 units at 2 m. Calculate the intensity at 6 m.
questions.explanation: Intensity obeys the inverse-square law. Going from 2 m to 6 m is ×3 the distance, so intensity falls by 3² = 9: 36/9 = 4 units. The same I ∝ 1/r² law applies to both sound and light. Dividing by 3 instead of 9 is the trap.

questions.q: For a combined process, identify which equation applies to each stage: melting ice at 0 °C, then warming the water to 100 °C.
options: Q = mcΔT for melting; Q = mL for warming
Q = mL for melting; Q = mcΔT for warming; then sum the stages
questions.explanation: Melting is a change of state at constant temperature → Q = mL (latent heat of fusion). Warming the water is a temperature change → Q = mcΔT. For a combined process you compute each stage and add them. Ask 'is the temperature changing, or the state?' to pick the equation.

questions.q: Explain, in terms of superposition, how a standing wave forms on a string.
options: A single wave reflects and changes frequency
Two identical waves travelling in opposite directions superpose, cancelling at nodes (always out of phase) and reinforcing at antinodes (always in phase)
Two waves of different frequencies beat together
A wave diffracts through the string
questions.explanation: Two identical waves (an incident wave and its reflection) travel in opposite directions and superpose. Where they are always out of phase the displacements cancel — nodes (zero displacement); where always in phase they reinforce — antinodes (maximum). The fixed pattern is a standing wave, which stores energy rather than transporting it.
